advanced.headnote: Week 4 continues with the same 5-7 days training schedule during which you will try to increase your weekly long run by a mile and add an additional repeat to the hill workout. This is the last week of hill repeats and fartlek runs. New speed workouts will begin next week.
advanced.footnote: Congratulations! You have just completed your first month of training. Six weeks to go before the Credit Union Cherry Blossom. Remember to stay hydrated before, during, and after your runs, even in cold weather.

days.tip: As your fitness level improves, you may feel that rest days are not as important. Resist this line of thought and enjoy the extra time (or sleep). If you want to stretch on your day off, first go for a walk to warm up your muscles. Never stretch cold muscles. Yoga or Pilates will help increase flexibility.

days.workoutDetails:
Find a hill 200-400 meters long. 
 Warmup: Easy 1 mile 
 Hills: Surge up the hill, keeping arms relaxed. Jog down, and repeat. 
 Cooldown: Easy 1 mile

Warmup: Easy 1 mile 
 Intervals: Pick up the pace to race pace (not a sprint) for 1 minute, jog 1 minute, pick up the pace for 2 minutes, jog 1 minute, pick up the pace for 3 minutes, jog 1 minute, etc, until you work up to 6 minutes. If you are feeling good, try to run 7 minutes at race pace. 
 Cooldown: Easy 1 mile
Going for a walk or easy bike ride can help work out muscle tightness to prepare for the weekend's hard work.

days.tip: Try to stay relaxed while running. Your arms should be relaxed and swinging like a pendulum. Keep your shoulders loose.

days.workoutDetails: Increase your weekly long run by 1 mile.

advanced.headnote: Week 5 continues with 5-7 days of running while maintaining the weekly long run. Speed sessions change to anaerobic threshold training, with one day of mile repeats and one day of an anaerobic threshold (tempo) run.

days.tip: Check your shoes for wear. It might be time to think about getting a new pair of shoes. Many runners have two pairs and alternate running in them. They always have an old pair already broken in and a new pair they are breaking in. Most shoes wear out in the mid-sole after 300-500 miles, even though the bottom of the shoe may not appear worn.

days.workoutDetails:
Warmup: Easy 1 mile 
 Intervals: 1 mile repeats. Your goal is to run a mile at race pace, or 80-85% of your maximum heart rate. Recovery jog for 2-3 minutes between repeats. 
 Cooldown: Easy 1 mile 
 If you have to stop and walk after the mile, you may be running the workout too fast. This should be a hard but sustainable effort.
Easy recovery run at 60-70%. You may feel stiff and sore from yesterday's mile repeats. It should become easier next week.

Warmup: Easy 1 mile 
 Anaerobic threshold: Run at your current race pace, or at 80-85% of your maximum heart rate, for 20-25 minutes. The effort should be somewhat hard - you should feel like you could keep it up for a long time but if you picked it up very much, you might feel like stopping. 
 Cooldown: Easy 1 mile

days.tip: Don't worry if your appetite is increasing. Your body needs more fuel because of increased mileage and intensity. You may need more complex carbohydrates, including whole grains and vegetables.
Remember to stretch after every run.

days.workoutDetails: Try 2-3 miles at race pace at the end of the long run to see what the effort feels like. Keep the rest of the run at a conversational pace, 90-120 minutes slower than race pace, or at 60-70%.

advanced.headnote: Week 6 continues with 5-7 days of running with a weekly long run and 2 anaerobic threshold speed sessions. 4 more weeks of hard training are ahead.

days.workoutDetails:
Warmup: Easy 1 mile 
 Intervals: 1 mile repeats at race pace, or 80-85% or maximum heartrate, with a 2-minute recovery. Your goal for the repeat miles is to run at your anaerobic threshold (15K or 10-mile race pace) with a very short recovery. 
 Cooldown: Easy 1 mile 
 If at the end of the mile repeat you have to walk, you are probably running the workout too hard.

days.tip: Try to find a course with rolling hills. The hills will make you stronger.

days.workoutDetails:
Warmup: Easy 1 mile 
 Anaerobic threshold: Run at your current race pace, or at 80-85% of your maximum heart rate, for 20-25 minutes. You should feel like you could keep going for a long time, but if you picked up the pace you would feel like stopping. 
 Cooldown: Easy 1 mile
Do not be concerned if you are still feeling tired and sore from the last 2 weeks of hard workouts.
Another easy recovery before Sunday's long run.
days.tip: Wear sunglasses with UV protection to keep your eyes safe on sunny days.

days.workoutDetails:
Increase your weekly long run by 1 mile. Keep your run at 60-75% effort. 
 If you are racing today, you can still get your long run in by doing a 2-3 mile warm up jog before the race, and a 2-3 mile cool down run after the race (depending on the length of the race).

advanced.headnote: Week 7 continues with 5-7 days of running with a weekly long run and 2 anaerobic threshold speed sessions. This is a good week to test your fitness level with a race on this weekend or next - the race should replace the 20-25 minute threshold run.

days.workoutDetails:
Warmup: Easy 1 mile 
 Intervals: Work on pacing. Run the first mile repeat at your race pace (80-85% of maximum heart rate). Try to run each mile 5 seconds faster than the previous mile. 
 Cooldown: Easy 1 mile

days.tip: Whenever possible, try to run on a soft path or trail.

days.workoutDetails:
Warmup: Easy 1 mile 
 Anaerobic threshold: Effort for the anaerobic part of the workout should be at race pace (80-85% of maximum heart rate). The effort should feel somewhat hard but doable. You should feel like you could keep going for a long time but if you picked up the pace you would beel like stopping. 
 Cooldown: Easy 1 mile 
 You should feel terrific within 30 minutes of completing the workout.

Rest days are very important for improving and maintaining fitness. During rest days or very easy recovery days, your body will assimilate the training that you have been doing, which makes you stronger and faster.

days.tip: If weight training is a regular part of your weekly activities, consider cutting back on your intensity and/or stopping altogether until after the race.

days.workoutDetails: Run 3 miles at your goal race pace during the middle of the long run, and the last mile, to see if you can handle the pace. Stay hydrated.

advanced.headnote: Week 8 is the last hard week of training of 5-7 days with a long run and 2 anaerobic threshold speed sessions. Try to increase your long run by a mile.

days.tip: If you feel tired from your training, you can always take an extra day off on Wednesday to let your body recover.

days.workoutDetails:
Warmup: Easy 1 mile 
 Intervals: Work on pacing. Run the first mile repeat at your race pace (80-85% of maximum heart rate). Try to run each successive mile 5 seconds faster than the previous mile. 
 Cooldown: Easy 1 mile

days.tip: Remember to stretch after each run.

days.workoutDetails:
Warmup: Easy 1 mile 
 Anaerobic threshold: Run nonstop at race pace for 20-25 minutes. Try to stay between 80-85% of maximum heart rate for the entire 20-25 minutes. The effort should be quite hard - you should feel like you could keep going for a long time but if you picked up the pace you would want to stop. 
 Cooldown: Easy 1 mile 
 You should feel terrific within 30 minutes of completing the workout.
Run 2 predicted race pace miles after you are 2 or 3 miles into the run and at the end of the run.

advanced.headnote:
Week 9 begins your taper. This week will be moderate in intensity. Your hard training is over and you should be doing a lot of easy running. Too much hard running before a long race can leave you tired on race day, with disappointing race results. 
 This week includes doing training at projected race pace. Training volume should be reduced by 20-25% with perhaps an extra rest day. The weekly long run should be reduced by 2-3 miles.

days.workoutDetails:
Warmup: Easy 1 mile 
 Intervals: 1 mile repeats at goal race pace with a 2-3 minute recovery. 
 Cooldown: Easy 1 mile

days.tip: Most of your training is over. You will start having more days off. Remember to stretch on days off.

days.workoutDetails:
Warmup: Easy 1 mile 
 Intervals: 1 mile repeats at goal race pace with a 2-3 minute recovery. 
 Cooldown: Easy 1 mile

days.tip: Nothing you do today will make you faster on race day. Overtraining, however, can result in a bad race. You want to go into the race rested and frisky.

days.workoutDetails: Run very easy.
Run 2-3 miles at predicted race pace at the end of the run. The more race pace miles you run, the more likely it is that you will be able to hold that pace for 10 miles.

advanced.headnote: Week 10 is a taper week with lots of rest in which weekly mileage is reduced by 50%. Your goal is to be fresh and fully recovered on race day. Easy days should be low mileage accompanied by rest days.

days.workoutDetails: Make sure you are stretching and staying hydrated. If you feel strong, you can run a mile at race pace followed by an easy mile and then a second mile at race pace.

days.tip: REST. It is important to stay hydrated during this taper.

days.workoutDetails: Very slow jog. This is one of your last easy runs before the race. You should go out very leisurely. Start mentally preparing for the race.

days.tip: Eat a nice pasta dinner. Lay out your running gear the night before the race and pin your number on your shirt.

Run easy for the first 10-15 minutes. AVOID GOING OUT TOO FAST. Zigzagging around other runners during the first mile when the course is the most crowded is counter-productive. The few seconds you save at the beginning may cost you minutes at the end of the race because of expending needless energy. Slowly pick up your pace until you are at your goal race pace by the end of the first mile. Try to run steady and comfortably. You can always pick up the pace for the last few miles. GOOD LUCK!

advanced.headnote: Easy recovery week with no speedwork. It takes about 10 days to fully recover from a 10-mile race. By the weekend, you should be able to do your normal long run. You can resume full all-out training and speedwork the following week. Keep up your weekly long run of 10-12 miles over the spring and summer. This will enable you to run a half-marathon or full marathon this fall.

intermediate.headnote: You are now beginning your fourth week of training. Daily runs continue to increase as does the weekly long run. You should start feeling stronger now and may want to increase the distance of your daily training run.
intermediate.footnote: Congratulations! You have just finished your first month of training. 6 weeks to go before the Credit Union Cherry Blossom.

days.tip: If you want to stretch today, first go for a walk or bike ride to warm up your muscles. Never stretch cold muscles! Find a yoga or pilates class to help with flexibility and core strength.

days.workoutDetails: Easy run to recover from Sunday's long run.
days.tip: A training partner can help you maintain your motivation. Contact your local running club or running store for help in finding a partner or a running group.

days.workoutDetails:
Warmup: Easy 1 mile 
 Intervals: Pick up the pace for 15 seconds (THIS IS NOT A SPRINT!), jog for 45 seconds, and repeat until you have run 2-3 miles. 
 Cooldown: Easy 1 mile 
 If you only ran 1-2 miles of intervals last week, try adding an additional mile of intervals this week. You should not be huffing and puffing or be out of breath during this workout. If you are, you may be running too hard. The purpose of this workout is to work on leg turnover and prepare you for the next few weeks when you will be running at a fast pace for a longer distance.

days.workoutOverview: Easy jog to recover from yesterday's workout.
days.tip: Always maintain a conversational pace when running on a recovery day. Keep the effort at 60-70% of your maximum heart rate, or 90-120 seconds per mile slower than you race pace.
Go for a walk to loosen up your legs to prepare for the weekend's hard work. Do some light stretching of your calves, hamstrings, and quadriceps.
Try to stay relaxed while running. Try to make your arms swing like a pendulum.

days.workoutDetails: ncrease your weekly long run by 1 mile.

intermediate.headnote: The intensity picks up this week and the speed workout changes. You will continue to increase your Sunday long run to prepare for the race.

days.tip: Check your shoes for wear. It might be time to think about getting a new pair of shoes. Many runners have 2 pairs and alternate running in them. They always have an old pair already broken in and a new pair they are breaking in. Many shoes wear out in the mid-sole after 300-500 miles, even though the bottom of the shoe does not show much wear.

days.workoutDetails: Run easy to recover from your longest run so far.
days.tip: While running, try to visualize yourself floating in the air. Stay relaxed and let your arms swing like a pendulum.

days.workoutDetails:
Warmup: Easy 1 mile 
 Intervals: Run 2 minutes hard, then jog for 2 minutes. Do this 8 times for a total of 32 minutes. 
 Cooldown: Easy 1 mile 
 The 2 minutes hard IS NOT A SPRINT. Try to run at your race effort. The effort should be somewhat difficult and involve some huffing and puffing, especially at the end of the workout.
days.tip: If you want an idea of how fast you are running, find a local track.

days.workoutDetails: Easy run to recover from yesterday's speed session.
days.tip: You may feel stiff and sore from yesterday's workout. The effort should become easier in the next few weeks.
Don't worry if your appetite is increasing. Your body needs more fuel because of increased mileage and intensity. Make sure your diet includes complex carbohydrates and try to avoid junk food.
Remember to stretch after every run.

For those with a specific time goal for the race, run a mile or 2 at your projected race pace near the end of the long run to see what the effort feels like during a long run. Keep the rest of the run at a conversational pace, or 60-75% of maximum heart rate.

intermediate.headnote: You are now entering your second half of the training program. You are on your way to a great 10-mile race.

days.tip: Take a look at how far you have come in 5 weeks. Congratulate yourself on your effort so far.
If you can run on a course with rolling hills, you will be getting a bonus because hills will make you stronger. Expect your heart rate to go above 75% on the uphill sections.

days.workoutDetails:
Warmup: Easy 1 mile 
 Intervals: Run 2 minutes hard, jog for 2 minutes. Do this 8 times for a total of 32 minutes. 
 Cooldown: Easy 1 mile 
 The 2-minute hard segments are NOT A SPRINT. Try to run them at your race effort. The effort should be somewhat difficult and involve some huffing and puffing, especially at the end of the workout.
Go easy to recover from yesterday's hard workout.
days.tip: Wear sunglasses with UV protection to keep your eyes safe on sunny days.
Do not be concerned if you are still feeling tired and sore from Wendesday's workout. That is a normal feeling.

days.workoutDetails: Easy recovery before Sunday's long run.
days.tip: Make sure you are getting enough sleep. Keep easy runs at a conversational effort.

days.workoutDetails: Increase your weekly long run by 1 mile. Keep it conversational. For those with a specific time goal for the race, run a mile or 2 at your projected race pace near the end of the long run to see what the effort feels like.
days.tip: For those of you dreaming of running a marathon, this 10-week program can be a springboard to a fall marathon.

intermediate.headnote: You are now beginning the last few weeks of hard training. Workouts continue to increase in distance, especially the long run. The long run should be getting easier now that you are getting in better shape. You might even notice that you have lost a couple of pounds.

days.tip: If weight training is a regular part of your weekly activities, consider cutting back on your intensity until race day.
Remember to stretch.

days.workoutDetails:
Warmup: Easy 1 mile 
 Intervals: Run 2 minutes hard, then jog for 2 minutes. 
 Cooldown: Easy 1 mile 
 The 2-minute hard segments ARE NOT A SPRINT. Instead, try to stay relaxed while working a bit harder. Try to run at your race effort (80-85% of maximum heart rate). The effort should be somewhat difficult and involve some huffing and puffing, expecially at the end of the workout.
Easy recovery after yesterday's hard workout.

days.tip: Rest days are important. Your body assimilates the training that you have been doing, helping to make you stronger and faster.
Every mile that you run will make you stronger to race.

days.workoutDetails: Increase your weekly long run by 1 mile. Run at a conversational effort. You only have 1 long run remaining on your training schedule. Try to run a couple predicted race pace miles during the middle of the long run to see how your body reacts to the pace.
days.tip: Continue to hydrate.

intermediate.headnote: This is the last week of hard training. The end is in sight. Continue to focus on the weekly long run. THe 2-minute interval runs continue.

days.workoutDetails: Easy run, especially if you increased the distance of your long run.
days.tip: Think how hard these runs used to be when you started training. Think how much easier your runs have become.

days.workoutDetails:
Warmup: Easy 1 mile 
 Intervals: Run 2 minutes hard, then jog for 2 minutes. Do this 10 times for a total of 40 minutes. 
 Cooldown: Easy 1 mile 
 The 2-minute hard segments are NOT A SPRINT. Instead, it is a surge in which you try to stay relaxed while working hard. Try to run at your race effort. The effort should be somewhat difficult and involve some huffing and puffing, especially at the end of the workout.
Easy recovery run to recover from your last speed session.
days.tip: Remember to stretch after each run.
Think about what clothing you are going to wear on race day.

days.workoutDetails: Try to run a couple of predicted race pace miles during the middle of the long run to see how your body reacts to the pace.

intermediate.headnote: You are now starting to taper. This week will be moderate in intensity. The last week is very easy. The hard part of your training has been completed and the remainder will consist primarily of easy running. Too much hard running the last couple of weeks before a long race can leave you tired on race day, with disappointing race results.

days.tip: Go for an easy walk or take a Yoga class to help your flexibility.

days.workoutDetails: Easy run at conversational pace.
days.tip: When tapering, it is normal to feel strong and frisky. Resist the urge to run too hard during the taper.

days.workoutDetails: Throw in 1 or 2 miles in the middle of the run at projected race pace to see how it feels.

days.tip: Most of your training is over. You will start having more days off. Remember to stretch on days off.

days.workoutDetails: Run 1 mile at your race pace to see how it feels when you are rested.
Optional easy run for those who have trained at the higher range of the Intermediate Training Program.
days.tip: You should start feeling stronger after these days off and/or easy days.

days.workoutDetails: Last long run. Consider including 2-3 miles at projected race pace at the end of the run. The more race-pace miles you run, the more you are likely to be able to hold that pace for 10 miles.

intermediate.headnote: This is your taper week, meaning very little running. There is no training benefit to running hard during the week before the race. The danger is not being sufficiently rested and being tired on race day.

days.tip: Less than 1 week to go. Your main goal now should be making sure you are fully rested on Sunday.
Make sure you are stretching and staying hydrated.
REST, REST, REST

days.workoutDetails: Very slow jog. This will be your last run until the race. You should go out very leisurely.
days.tip: Start mentally preparing for the race.
You can do an optional 2-3 mile run at a very easy effort. You should go our very leisurely.
Eat a nice pasta dinner. Lay out your running gear the night before the race and pin your number on your shirt.

Run easy for the first 10-15 minutes. AVOID GOING OUT TOO FAST. Zigzagging around other runners during the first mile when the course is the most crowded is counter-productive. The few seconds you save at the beginning may cost you minutes at the end of the race because of expending needless energy. Slowly pick up your pace until you are at your goal race pace by the end of the first mile. Try to run steady and comfortably. You can always pick up the pace for the last few miles. GOOD LUCK!

intermediate.headnote:
Do not race this week and do not do any speedwork. You can resume your long run of 6-10 miles the following Sunday. After 10-14 days, you can resume normal training. Remember: because you race 10 miles, you need to take it easy for 10 days to let your body fully recover. 
 You now have the training base to run a fall half-marathon or even a full marathon. The key to staying in shape is to continue a weekly long run. Many local running clubs have group marathon training programs if you are interested.
